title: Broken Access Control
description: Access control enforces policy so users cannot act outside of their intended permissions.
rationale: Failures in access control can let attackers view or modify data and perform unauthorized actions.
audit_procedure: Review authorization checks on sensitive routes, object access, and privilege boundaries.
remediation: Deny by default, enforce server-side authorization checks, validate object ownership, and log access denials.

title: Cryptographic Failures
description: Weak or missing cryptography can expose sensitive data in transit or at rest.
rationale: Improper data protection can lead to compromise of credentials, tokens, and personal data.
audit_procedure: Review TLS usage, key management, certificate handling, and storage of sensitive data.
remediation: Use strong TLS, protect keys, avoid storing sensitive data unnecessarily, and encrypt where required.

title: Injection
description: Injection flaws occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query.
rationale: Injection can allow attackers to read or modify data and execute unintended commands.
audit_procedure: Review database access patterns, input validation, and unsafe command construction.
remediation: Use parameterized queries, validate input, escape output where relevant, and avoid unsafe dynamic execution.

title: Insecure Design
description: Insecure design represents flaws in architecture and business logic that cannot be fixed by implementation alone.
rationale: Weak design choices can leave critical application flows vulnerable even if code quality is good.
audit_procedure: Review threat models, trust boundaries, abuse cases, and security requirements in design.
remediation: Use secure design patterns, threat modeling, and defensive workflows for critical user journeys.

title: Security Misconfiguration
description: Misconfigured services, headers, permissions, and defaults can expose applications to compromise.
rationale: Unsafe defaults and missing hardening create easy attack paths.
audit_procedure: Review headers, server settings, unnecessary features, error handling, and deployment defaults.
remediation: Harden defaults, disable unused features, set secure headers, and review deployment configuration regularly.

title: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
description: Using software with known vulnerabilities or that is unsupported increases the attack surface.
rationale: Exploiting known vulnerabilities in libraries or frameworks is a common and effective attack vector.
audit_procedure: Inventory all components/versions (SCA), check against vulnerability databases, and review EOL status.
remediation: Remove unused dependencies, use automated tools to monitor vulnerabilities, and patch or upgrade regularly.

title: Identification and Authentication Failures
description: Authentication and session weaknesses can let attackers compromise accounts and abuse login flows.
rationale: Weak authentication controls increase risk of credential stuffing, brute force, and account takeover.
audit_procedure: Review login protections, MFA, session handling, password controls, and lockout mechanisms.
remediation: Use MFA, rate limiting, secure session handling, strong password policies, and account lockout or delays.

title: Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: Failures in verifying software updates, data, and CI/CD pipelines can lead to unauthorized changes.
rationale: Insecure deserialization and unverified updates can lead to Remote Code Execution (RCE) or system compromise.
audit_procedure: Review update mechanisms, use of digital signatures, and deserialization of untrusted data.
remediation: Use digital signatures, verify integrity of CI/CD pipelines, and avoid deserializing untrusted data.

title: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
description: Insufficient logging and monitoring can delay detection and response to attacks.
rationale: Without reliable logs and alerting, incidents may go unnoticed or be harder to investigate.
audit_procedure: Review application logs, security events, alerting paths, and retention settings.
remediation: Enable meaningful security logging, centralize monitoring, protect log integrity, and alert on suspicious activity.

title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: SSRF occurs when a web application fetches a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL.
rationale: SSRF can allow attackers to bypass firewalls or reach internal systems and metadata services.
audit_procedure: Identify all functions that accept URLs, test for access to local/internal IP addresses and cloud metadata.
remediation: Use allow-lists for domains/protocols, sanitize input, and enforce network-level isolation for the application server.

title: Improper Inventory Management
description: Failure to maintain an accurate inventory of assets can lead to unpatched vulnerabilities and attack paths.
rationale: Unknown or unmanaged assets are often the weakest link in security defenses.
audit_procedure: Review asset inventories, perform network scans, and identify shadow IT or forgotten services.
remediation: Maintain an up-to-date inventory of all assets, including third-party services, and ensure they are included in security monitoring and patching processes.

title: Audit Logging of Security Events
description: Ensure all security-relevant events—including successful and failed logins, password changes, and privilege escalations—are logged with sufficient metadata.
rationale: Without a detailed audit trail, it is impossible to reconstruct the timeline of a breach or identify compromised accounts.
audit_procedure: Verify that logs include timestamp, source IP, User-Agent, User ID, and the success/failure status of the event.
remediation: Implement a centralized logging module that captures all authentication and authorization events in a structured format like JSON.

title: Log Integrity and Tamper Protection
description: Protect log files from unauthorized modification or deletion by attackers attempting to hide their tracks.
rationale: If an attacker gains administrative access, their first move is often to clear logs. Immutable logs ensure evidence is preserved.
audit_procedure: Check if logs are stored on a separate, write-once or append-only centralized log server (SIEM) rather than just the local web server.
remediation: Stream logs in real-time to a secure central server; use digital signatures or file integrity monitoring (FIM) to detect log tampering.

title: Prevention of Sensitive Data in Logs
description: Ensure that sensitive information such as clear-text passwords, session tokens, and PII are masked or excluded from logs.
rationale: Logs are often accessible to developers or third-party tools; storing secrets in logs creates a secondary data breach risk.
audit_procedure: Perform a keyword search in log repositories for patterns matching 'password', 'session_id', 'bearer', or 'credit_card'.
remediation: Use log scrubbing libraries to automatically redact sensitive fields before they are written to the disk or stream.
references: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

title: Real-time Monitoring and Alerting Thresholds
description: Establish automated alerts for suspicious patterns, such as brute-force attempts or rapid data exfiltration.
rationale: Logging is passive; monitoring is active. Alerts allow teams to respond to an attack while it is still in progress.
audit_procedure: Review alert configurations to ensure thresholds exist (e.g., '10 failed logins from 1 IP in 1 minute') and that they trigger notifications to the SOC.
remediation: Integrate log streams with an alerting engine (like ELK Watcher or Azure Monitor) to flag anomalies and high-severity errors immediately.
